serving.natural_language_portion: One large live crab, typically weighing 800-1000g.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, blue, raw
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data for raw blue crab used as a representative for live crab.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Protein-Rich Foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Crab is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food. Glycemic index is estimated to be very low, set to 1 per convention for non-glycemic foods, as it contains no significant glycemic carbohydrates.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume crab in moderation, especially when prepared with high-sodium sauces or seasonings, to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Crab is a suitable food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate and sugar content, provided it is not prepared with sugary sauces or batters.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While crab contains cholesterol, it is also a source of lean protein and beneficial minerals; moderation is advised, particularly for preparations high in saturated fat or sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Live Crab, Extra Large, refers to a large, fresh crab typically weighing between 800-1000g per piece, often found in Chinese cuisine, particularly in Singapore. Known for its succulent, sweet meat, it is a highly prized seafood delicacy. Common preparation methods include steaming, boiling, stir-frying with various sauces (e.g., chili crab, black pepper crab), or baking. Nutritionally, crab is an excellent source of lean protein, Vitamin B12, selenium, and zinc, while being very low in carbohydrates. It contains some cholesterol and sodium, which should be considered for individuals managing cardiovascular health or hypertension. Due to its negligible carbohydrate content, it is a suitable protein source for those managing Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: Nutrient data for raw crab sourced from USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate based on the food's macronutrient profile. Multilingual names and aliases are researched for Singaporean context.
notes: Carbohydrate_g, sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g, and trans_fat_g are naturally zero as crab meat contains no significant amounts of these nutrients. Vitamin A (RAE) is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. All other nutrients are reported from the source.
